coredata.dc:title: Effects of functional carbon nanodots on water hyacinth response to Cd/Pb stress: Implication for phytoremediation
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Qiong
Cao, Xiufeng
Liu, Beibei
Nie, Xiangkun
Liang, Taibo
Suhr, Jonghwan
Ci, Lijie
coredata.dc:description: Phytoremediation is one of the effective, economic and green approaches to cope with the increasing worldwide heavy metal (HM) pollution. Here, we evaluate the effects of functional carbon nanodots (FCNs) against the hyperaccumulation capacity as well as the physiological and genetic responses of water hyacinth under Pb2+ or/and Cd2+ stress. The bioaccumulation efficiency, HM content and transfer factor, biomass, root development, chlorophyll content, antioxidant system and genes expression are investigated at various concentration of HMs. Based on the excellent adsorption capacity and plant growth regulation ability, FCNs and nitrogen doped FCNs (N-FCNs) cooperate with water hyacinth to improve their HMs removal efficiencies. FCNs and N-FCNs immobilize excess HMs ions in plant, smartly regulate enzymatic levels to mitigate oxidative damage, as well as regulate the microelement uptake and related gene expression, thus improve plant tolerance against HMs stress. Although Pb and Cd have antagonistic effects on bioaccumulation of water hyacinth to the single metal, FCNs and N-FCNs can cooperate with water hyacinth to raise the removal efficiency of HMs in water, and enhance plant tolerance under Pb–Cd combined stress. The promotion effects of FCNs and N-FCNs on phytoremediation are more effective than conventional carbon nanomaterials, including carbon nanotubes and graphene oxides. These findings demonstrate that the application of FCNs or N-FCNs can improve the phytoremediation efficiency in the restoration of HMs contaminated water area. This study provides important insights into the possibility of using FCNs-based nanomaterials and water hyacinth as synergistic system for remediation of Cd–Pb contaminated water area.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Phytoremediation is one of the effective, economic and green approaches to cope with the increasing worldwide heavy metal (HM) pollution. Here, we evaluate the effects of functional carbon nanodots (FCNs) against the hyperaccumulation capacity as well as the physiological and genetic responses of water hyacinth under Pb2+ or/and Cd2+ stress. The bioaccumulation efficiency, HM content and transfer factor, biomass, root development, chlorophyll content, antioxidant system and genes expression are investigated at various concentration of HMs. Based on the excellent adsorption capacity and plant growth regulation ability, FCNs and nitrogen doped FCNs (N-FCNs) cooperate with water hyacinth to improve their HMs removal efficiencies. FCNs and N-FCNs immobilize excess HMs ions in plant, smartly regulate enzymatic levels to mitigate oxidative damage, as well as regulate the microelement uptake and related gene expression, thus improve plant tolerance against HMs stress. Although Pb and Cd have antagonistic effects on bioaccumulation of water hyacinth to the single metal, FCNs and N-FCNs can cooperate with water hyacinth to raise the removal efficiency of HMs in water, and enhance plant tolerance under Pb–Cd combined stress. The promotion effects of FCNs and N-FCNs on phytoremediation are more effective than conventional carbon nanomaterials, including carbon nanotubes and graphene oxides. These findings demonstrate that the application of FCNs or N-FCNs can improve the phytoremediation efficiency in the restoration of HMs contaminated water area. This study provides important insights into the possibility of using FCNs-based nanomaterials and water hyacinth as synergistic system for remediation of Cd–Pb contaminated water area.

ce:section.ce:para: Aquatic environmental deterioration caused by rapid industry development, especially heavy metal (HM) pollution, has become a severe global challenge today (Du et al., 2020). HM contaminants, as the major environmental pollutants worldwide, can cause serious, irreversible health disorders to plants, animals and humans (Gao et al., 2020). Cadmium (Cd) and lead (Pb) are such metals that are highly toxic either in their elemental form or as soluble (Romero-Hernández et al., 2017). Furthermore, they tend to be bioaccumulated and biomagnified at the toxic level through the food chain and impose environmental and health risks (Xu et al., 2020). Some conventional techniques have been used to remove the toxic pollutants from polluted water, such as chemical precipitation, filtration, adsorption, ion exchange, reverse osmosis, etc. (Chen et al., 2018; Kumar et al., 2020) Usually, these processes are effective in removing most of the HMs from wastewater with moderate to high concentrations. However, they are either inefficient or unprofitable when applied to HM polluted water at low concentrations. Moreover, most of the measures are costly, lack of selectivity and even cause secondary pollution to the environment (Alaboudi et al., 2018; Song et al., 2019). Therefore, there is an urgent need for an economical, efficient, selective and environmentally friendly process to cope with HMs pollution. Hyperaccumulator can be used to prevent the dispersal of HMs and remediate contaminated sites without substantially disturbing the environment (Du et al., 2020). In this regard, phytoremediation is a promising technique to remove, degrade or immobilize metal ions from water or soils by plants.

Water hyacinth (Eichhornia crassipes), which has been known to extract toxins from waterways that they are planted within, has gained widespread interest in aquatic environmental phytoremediation application (Zheng et al., 2016). Water hyacinth is widespread, fast-growing, tolerant to HMs and able to efficiently accumulate varieties of HMs including Cd, Pb, Cu, Cr, Zn, Sn, Ag, etc. (Du et al., 2020; Zheng et al., 2016) Although water hyacinth can quickly remediate low-concentration sewage, the efficiency is low in the case of high concentration. Considering the time-consuming process of phytoremediation, the combination of nanotechnology and phytoremediation is applied to further improve the remediation efficiency (Song et al., 2019).

Nanomaterials can function in the phytoremediation system through directly removing pollutants, promoting plant growth, and increasing pollutant phytoavailability (Song et al., 2019). Carbon nanomaterials (CNMs) possess dramatically immobilization effect on HMs by adsorption-precipitation, due to their large surface area and multiple functional groups on the surface or at the edge of the layers (Gusain et al., 2020). Numerous studies have clearly demonstrated that CNMs, including carbon nanotubes (CNTs), graphene-based materials, biochar, etc., had superior adsorption capacity and high adsorption efficiencies for various HMs, such as Pb, Cd, Ni, Cu and Cr (Huang et al., 2020; Peng et al., 2020; Rebekah et al., 2020; Tian et al., 2020; Vikrant et al., 2019; Ihsanullah et al., 2016; Sadeghinya et al., 2015) In addition, CNMs showed good biocompatibility to plant by promoting growth, activating root uptake and enhancing stress resistance (Swift et al., 2020; Asgher et al., 2021; Gong et al., 2020). The interactions between CNMs and aquatic macrophyte are likely to influence the uptake of HMs in plants and plants' responses to metal-induced toxicity. For example, CNTs were demonstrated to not only improve total Cd accumulation by delivering ions into plant organs, but also mitigate Cd ions toxicity to hyperaccumulator (Liu et al., 2020). Nevertheless, in another study, although CNTs can facilitate the phytoremediation of Cd, they also aggravate metal-induced toxicity to Boehmeria nivea (L.) (Gong et al., 2020). Due to the uncertainty of impacts on plants, as well as the high cost and their potential biotoxicity, the practical application of traditional CNMs in environment is restricted. Therefore, new functional CNMs with low-cost, better biosafety and universality are in urgent need of facilitating phytoremediation to cope with HM pollution. In our previous researches, functional carbon nanodots (FCNs) and nitrogen-doped FCNs (N-FCNs) with high hydrophilicity and biocompatibility were synthesized by a green and cheap electrolysis process, and can effectively promote plant (Arabidopsis, pakchoi, tomato and soybean) growth and tolerance to ions (Chen et al., 2020b, 2021). The carboxyl, ester and amino groups on the surface of FCNs-based nanomaterials showed better effects on the plant growth regulation, owing to that they can improve nanomaterials’ aqueous dispersity, nutrient adsorption capacity and the affinity to biomacromolecules (Chen et al., 2020a, 2020c). Based on the high adsorption performance and the bioaffinity, they can regulate the rhizosphere pH and transport Cd2+ into plant, thus significantly improving plant bioaccumulation capacity for Cd2+. Simultaneously, N-FCNs alleviate the toxicity caused by high Cd2+ stress on plant by increasing the biomass, complexing ions, and reducing the oxidative damage (Chen et al., 2020a, 2020b). However, these previous studies focused on laboratory theoretical researches on Arabidopsis thaliana (A. thaliana) and crops, and lack of studies on the synergistic effects of FCNs-based nanomaterials on aquatic plant, as well as researches on hyperaccumulation of macrophyte and practical applications in environment.

Therefore, this study aims to expand the environmental applications of FCNs-based nanomaterials and explore their potential capacity by examining the interaction between FCN (or N-FCNs) and Cd2+ or (and) Pb2+ according molecular dynamics simulation, investigating the effects of FCNs (or N-FCNs) on the bioaccumulation and tolerance of water hyacinth to HMs, clarifying the regulation mechanism in molecular level, and evaluating their biosecurity to environment. Plant biomass, HMs contents and distribution, pigment levels, oxidative stress, antioxidant enzymes (including superoxide dismutase (SOD), peroxidase (POD), and catalase (CAT)), nutrient uptake as well as gene expression were investigated. The performance of FCNs-based materials on phytoremediation were also compared with CNTs and graphene oxide (GO). This study will provide new insights into the interaction among FCNs-based nanomaterials, plants, and metal-contaminants, which is likely to explore a potential novel application of FCNs-based nanomaterials for the environmental remediation. Overall, study on this topic is of interest both in theory and practice.
The detail materials and reagents information are supplemented in the supplementary materials (SMs).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of FCNs-based materials
ce:section.ce:para: FCNs and N-FCNs were synthesized according to the previous reported electrolysis method (Chen et al., 2020b, 2020c). The details of the synthesis and characterization processes are listed in the SMs.

The adsorption isotherms of FCNs and N-FCNs were evaluated by fitting the experimental data to the nonlinearized forms of Langmuir isotherm (Langmuir, 1918). Equilibrium concentration (Ce) was used to calculate the equilibrium capacity of adsorption (qe). The details of the adsorption experiments can be found in the SMs.

Pb2+ and Cd2+ are wide spread pollutants in the environment (Saleh et al., 2020). The experiments were designed as Table S1, and exhibited detailly in the SMs. In the experiments, Pb2+ concentration in water was set as 0.1, 1.0, 10, 30, 50 and 100 mg L−1, while Cd2+ concentration was 0.01, 0.1 and 10 mg L−1. According to the maximum concentration of sewage prescribed by national standard for wastewater discharge guideline (GB8978-1996) of China, the concentration of Pb2+ and Cd2+ in their combined polluted water was 1.0 and 0.1 mg L−1, respectively. The additive amounts of FCNs or N-FCNs were 1, 4, 8, 16, 30 mg L−1. Besides, CNTs and GO of 4 mg L−1 were set for comparisons. Model organism A. thaliana was chosen to study the molecular biology of plant adsorption and tolerance to HMs.

The HM content in polluted water was tested after filtration by an inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-AES, Optima 8000). Removal efficiency was calculated as Equation (1).
(1)
Removal efficiency
( % )
= （
C 0
−
C f
） /
C 0
× 100
where
C f
and
C 0
are the HMs concentrations after and before adsorption, respectively.

The HMs levels in each part of dry plant were also determined by ICP-AES after digestion (details are shown in the SMs). Transfer factor (TF) from root to aerial portion was calculated according to Equation (2).
(2)
TF =
C A
/
C R
where
C A
and
C R
are the metal concentration in the aerial portion and root, respectively.
Biomass of plants, genes expression, as well as the pigments, antioxidants and nutrient ions in seedlings were determined by the methods listed in SMs.

All of the collected statistics were presented as an arithmetic means and their corresponding standard deviations (SDs) of at least 3 replicates. Differences between treatments were statistically analyzed using one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) with the software Origin 8.0. According to Tukey's multiple comparisons tests, the significant differences (P < 0.05) were labeled with different letters.

In the previous reports (Zhou et al., 2007; Li et al., 2021), saline or acid solution were used as electrolytes in electrolysis process to acquire carbon nanodots. The products require further purification before they can be applied to natural environment. However, the well soluble FCNs and N-FCNs were obtained by the green electrolysis method with no reagent loss, which is more effective and economical. The raw materials costs to produce 1 ton of FCNs and N-FCNs sols (The solid contents are 3.6 kg and 5.4 kg, respectively.) were 35.4 dollars and 44.5 dollars, respectively (Details in SMs and Table S2). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) analysis and Atomic Force Microscope (AFM) indicate that FCNs have smaller size than N-FCNs, when FCNs are dots of 2–8 nm and N-FCNs are 4–10 nm (Fig. 1a, S1). Raman spectra in Fig. 1 b indicate that plenty of nitrogen-containing functional groups are connected to the carbon skeleton of N-FCNs and form more sp3 hybrid bonds with disordered structure than FCNs, which leaded to the higher degree of disorder in N-FCNs. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra (Fig. 1c) of lyophilized N-FCNs and FCNs show that the main functional groups of N-FCNs are –OH (3160 cm−1), –NH– (-NH4, –NH2, 3408 cm−1), C–N (1337 cm−1), –COO– (1585 cm−1), OC-R (around 1700 cm−1), –CH2- (1403 cm−1), C–O–C (1260 cm−1), etc., while FCNs have groups including –COOH, –COOR, –OH, OC-R, C–O–C, etc. Compared to the carbon nanodots reported in other studies (Zhang et al., 2020; Li et al., 2021), FCNs and N-FCNs have more hydrophilic groups and electronegative groups, such as –OH, –COOH, –COO– and –NH2.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) survey spectra of FCNs and N-FCNs are presented in Fig. S2. The relative atom content of oxygen to carbon is 1.7: 1 in FCNs, and the relative atom content of O: N: C is 2.1: 0.6: 1 by calculation. It also reflects that more oxygen-containing and nitrogen-containing groups are in N-FCNs than FCNs. The bonding information of C1s and N1s from high-resolution XPS spectra (Fig. 1d–f) is consistent with the FTIR results. The main functional groups on FCNs are O–CO (at 288.9 eV), C–O-R (H) (at 286.7 eV) and sp 2 C (at 284.5 eV) with relative content of 0.6: 0.05: 1.0. By comparation, C-OR/C–OH and COO peaks are absent in C1s spectrum of FCNs, while the relative content of CO, C–N, sp 2 C is about 1.8: 1.0: 5.0. The XPS N1s spectra of N-FCNs can be deconvoluted into two components centered at 400.9 eV and 399.0 eV, corresponding to the ammonium and amino, respectively, and their content ratio is about 1.4: 1. Because of the large quantity of electronegative functional groups, the zeta potential is negative for both FCNs (−25.1 mV) and N-FCNs (−18.9 mV) sols at pH 6.8 (Fig. S3). Therefore, FCNs and N-FCNs are carbon-based nanodots with high content of electronegative functional groups, which will endow them high adsorption capacity for HMs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption capacity of FCNs and N-FCNs on HMs
ce:section.ce:para: Due to their unique structural properties, FCNs-based materials are inclined to interact with contaminants and nutrients in aqueous environment, and they can be sequentially aggregated or precipitated at the container bottom or the surface of plant roots (Chen et al., 2020a). The facilitation of HMs absorption may influence their environmental behavior to the aquatic macrophytes. To investigate the adsorption performance of FCNs and N-FCNs, Langmuir isotherm model is employed to correlate the experimental data obtained at equilibrium (Egbosiuba and Abdulkareem, 2020). Based on the values of R2, Langmuir model can well describe the adsorption isotherms of Pb2+ and Cd2+ onto FCNs and N-FCNs (Fig. 2 a and b). It affirms that Pb2+ and Cd2+ are absorbed by the specific sites of FCNs or N-FCNs, indicating their chemisorption behavior of the adsorption to HMs (Tian et al., 2020). Functional groups on FCNs and N-FCNs adsorb Pb2+ and Cd2+ mainly through ion exchange, complexation and electrostatic attraction, which caused FCNs-based materials to be folded and form large aggregates (Chen et al., 2020a). The theoretical saturated adsorption capacity (qm) of FCNs to Pb2+ and Cd2+ is 1346.3 and 697.0 mg g−1, respectively, while that of N-FCNs to Pb2+ and Cd2+ is 1620.9 and 773.4 mg g−1, respectively. After adsorption, the fluorescence intensity of FCNs-HMs and N-FCNs-HMs are sharply quenched, mainly because that the chromogenic groups on FCNs or N-FCNs are occupied and the chelates are too large (Fig. 2c). The zeta potentials of the FCNs-HMs and N-FCNs-HMs are increased to −12.4 and −13.2 eV, revealing the occupation of the electronegative groups by HMs. HMs ions can be removed by binding with carboxyl, hydroxyl, amide, amine and aromatic groups on the adsorbents through chemical and physical adsorption (Feng et al., 2020). The main changes in FTIR spectra (Fig. 2d) of FCNs and N-FCNs before and after absorbing Pb2+ or Cd2+ are reflected on the decreasing intensity or blue shift of carboxyl (1720 cm−1), hydroxyl (2900 - 3400 cm−1) and amino (3408, 1337 cm−1) absorption peaks. Pb4f and Cd3d XPS spectra (Fig. 2e and f) show that Pb and Cd in chelates are divalent. Compared to the XPS spectra of pure Pb2+ and Cd2+ in Fig. S4, the binding energy of Pb in chelates is higher. This demonstrates that FCNs and N-FCNs can act as electron donor when combining with Pb2+ ions. Compared with FCNs, carboxyl and amino groups further improve N-FCNs’ adsorption capacity to Pb2+ and Cd2+ by 20% and 10%, respectively. The above results indicate that more functional groups, especially electronegative groups, could increase the binding sites and adsorption efficiency of HMs on FCNs-based materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of FCNs-based materials on water hyacinth growth and bioaccumulation to HMs
ce:section.ce:para: The accumulation of metals in plants depends mainly on two aspects, i.e. metal concentration and plant biomass (Gong et al., 2020). FCNs and N-FCNs, which have unique properties to plant and HMs, may act as exogenous signals to affect the bioaccumulation and growth of water hyacinth either directly or indirectly via regulating physiological activities or altering the pollutants behavior. From Fig. 3 a, the shoot Pb content of water hyacinth increased with the increasing Pb2+ concentration from 0.1 to 100 mg L−1 in water, while the root Pb content shows highest at 30 mg L−1. The root Pb contents are higher than the shoot Pb contents below 30 mg L−1 of Pb in water, but the opposite is true above 50 mg L−1. The results reveal that water hyacinth mainly accumulates Pb in root at lower Pb2+ stress, but fail to absorb excessive Pb beyond 50 mg L−1. This is partly because that the physiological function of roots is seriously restricted by the high Pb stress, which is reflected on the decreased root length and biomass (Fig. S7&9). Cd contents are increased both in shoots and roots with the increasing Cd2+ concentration from 0.01 to 10 mg L−1, indicating that water hyacinth has good bioaccumulation and transport capacity for Cd at this concentration range.

At very low concentration (0.1, 1.0 mg L−1) of Pb2+, the significant lower Pb contents in vivo treated by FCNs or N-FCNs partly result from the dilution effect of the increasing biomass (Fig. 3g&S6). As a result, the removal efficiencies between the groups are of insignificant difference (Table S5). The TFs in Pb0.1F and Pb0.1N are above 1.5 times as high as Pb0.1 (Table S6), reflecting the improved ability of the plant for HMs uptake and transportation (Alaboudi et al., 2018). Firstly, this is partly because that FCNs and N-FCNs could both act as the transportation tools to promote HMs uptake and transfer in the plant, which can be proved by the similar ionic states of HMs detected by XPS in plant and chelates (Fig. S8). Secondly, the FCNs-based materials can induce vigorous roots (Fig. S9) and promote the root absorptive capacity (Chen et al., 2021). Besides, they can promote the transpiration of leaves to accelerate ions transport with water (Chen et al., 2020a). As N-FCNs can combine and deliver more Pb2+ to the plant because of their stronger HMs adsorption capacity compared to FCNs, they increase the Pb2+ TF at 1.0 mg L−1. Pb2+ accumulation rate in root is much higher than its transfer rate from root to shoot at 1.0 mg L−1 of Pb2+, which leads to the sharply decreased TFs compared to that at 0.1 mg L−1. When the Pb2+ concentration is 10 mg L−1, the growth rate of the plant treated by FCNs is about 2.7 times higher than control, while N-FCNs treatment has no significant difference from the control (Fig. 3g). Pb contents in shoots decrease to 66.9% and 52.1% with FCNs and N-FCNs treatment, while those in roots increase to 1.3 and 1.4 times, respectively (Fig. 3a, Table S3-4). As a result, BFs in Pb10F and Pb10N decrease more than 50% compared to Pb10 (Table S6). The reason is speculated as the large FCNs (or N-FCNs)-Pb chelates negatively affecting the ions transfer in the plant (Chen et al., 2020a).

It is interesting to find that FCNs and N-FCNs improved the removal efficiencies most significantly when Pb2+ concentration is 30 mg L−1, despite they decrease the Pb2+ contents in the root and shoot, as well as the FWs of water hyacinth. To explore the reason, the adsorption process of water hyacinth with time in Pb30, Pb30F and Pb30N was studied. Fig. 3c shows that the removal efficiencies of Pb30F and Pb30N on the 3rd day are much higher than that of Pb30. Therefore, the much higher level of Pb2+ in vivo strongly retards the growth of water hyacinth in the last four days in the treatment of Pb30F and Pb30N. Fig. 3c also shows that the initial bioaccumulation efficiency of water hyacinth for Pb is very fast. Although the final removal efficiencies in Pb30N and Pb30F are higher than Pb30, those are lower on 1-day. It may be because that the chelate of FCNs (or N-FCNs) and Pb2+ is larger than single ion at the high concentration of Pb2+, which makes the uptake of Pb by the root being slowly. After 1-day, FCNs and N-FCNs accelerate the passive absorption rate for Pb by carrying Pb into plant. Under 30 mg L−1 Pb2+ stress, Pb TFs (Table S6) in FCNs and N-FCNs treated groups are both reduced, and the Pb TFs treated by N-FCNs reduce more. It is because that the N-FCNs-Pb chelates are harder to be transferred in the plant than the FCNs-Pb chelates due to their larger size. The results prove that FCNs and N-FCNs can effectively improve the removal efficiency of Pb2+ in water hyacinth in short contact time.

The inhibitory effects of Pb2+ on plant growth are aggravated by FCNs and N-FCNs at 50 mg L−1 of Pb2+, mainly because the increased Pb content in plant (Fig. 3a, g). According to the higher TF, FCNs have advantage on transferring Pb ions in plant at high concentration of Pb2+ compared to N-FCNs (Table S4). Under the strongest Pb2+ poisoning (100 mg L−1), FW retardation caused by Pb toxicity is alleviated by N-FCNs because of the decreased Pb content in vivo. The reasons are ascribed to that N-FCNs in water absorb masses of Pb2+ and form large N-FCNs-Pb chelates, which are too large to be absorbed by water hyacinth roots.

The performance of water hyacinth treated by FCNs/N-FCNs under Cd stress is described minutely in SMs. FCNs can significantly suppress Cd toxicity and promote Cd2+ transfer in water hyacinth at 0.01 and 0.1 mg L−1 of Cd2+, while N-FCNs amplify the Cd toxicity at 0.1 and 1.0 mg L−1 of Cd2+ but promote Cd2+ removal at 1.0 mg L−1 of Cd2+ (Fig. 3, Table S5). The removal efficiency under 0.1 mg L−1 Cd2+ stress is much higher than that at the higher (10 mg L−1) or lower concentration (0.01 mg L−1) of Cd2+. The reasons are speculated as: on one hand, the Cd2+ concentration gradient inside and outside root is small at the lower concentration, which inhibits the transmembrane transport of Cd ions. On the other hand, the high level of Cd2+ stress can induce serious toxicity to the plant with reducing their biomass (Fig. 3g). What's more, the high initial Pb2+ concentration also decreases the removal efficiency.

Both of FCNs and N-FCNs in the wide concentration range (0–30 mg L−1) are biologically friendly for water hyacinth, although they show obvious dose-effect relationship on the bioaccumulation and growth of water hyacinth under the high concentration of Pb2+ and Cd2+ stress, which are shown in SMs and Fig. S10. Pb (Ⅱ) and Cd (Ⅱ) removal efficiency in the synergistic system of the plants and nanomaterials are maximumly increased by 21.3% and 41.9% with the FCNs addition of 8 mg L−1.

The above results suggest that FCNs and N-FCNs can facilitate the removal efficiency of HMs by water hyacinth in a short contact time, however, amplify the HMs toxicity to the plant over a longer period of time. The biotoxicity of lower levels of HMs is moderated, reflecting on the increased biomass and the well-developed root system. The removal efficiency of water hyacinth under the lower HMs stress is also promoted by FCNs or N-FCNs, which is partly due to the adsorption and transfer function of FCNs or N-FCNs, as well as the promotion effects of the vigorous roots on the HMs adsorption (Fig. S9). At the high concentrations of HMs (C Pb(Ⅱ) > 50 mg L−1), FCNs and N-FCNs, especially FCNs, can induce the plant absorb more HMs into the plant. However, this is at the expense of the significantly decreased biomass resulted from the serious toxicity of the high HMs level. In regard to the TFs of HMs in water hyacinth, TFs can be obviously raised and reduced at the lower and higher concentration of HMs, respectively. On one hand, FCNs and N-FCNs can not only act as the transportation but also promote the transpiration to accelerate the ions transfer from the root to the shoot. On the other hand, FCNs and N-FCNs can immobilize HMs ions in the plant and inhibit their transfer between plant organs (Chen et al., 2020b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of FCNs/N-FCNs on pigment and antioxidant system
ce:section.ce:para: The potential role of FCNs and N-FCNs in HMs toxicity to water hyacinth was further investigated with pigment level and antioxidant system. In general, HMs induce chlorophyll loss and disorder of the chloroplast function by damaging chloroplast ultrastructure, disturbing electrons transportation and damaging stomatal operating system (Mehmood et al., 2018). After 7-day exposure, both of FCNs and N-FCNs significantly promote the chlorophyll synthesis in normal condition or under Pb2+ (1 mg L−1) or Cd2+ (0.1 mg L−1) stress (Fig. 3h), probably because that CNMs could enter the leaves and increase the expression of genes associated with the chloroplast development and cell sizes (Zhang et al., 2017; Lian et al., 2020). Our previous study also found that FCNs-based nanomaterials could increase leaves thickness, thus increase the pigment level (Chen et al., 2020c).

Plant exposed to Pb and Cd always exhibits unnecessary increased malonaldehyde (MDA) level, which is used as a biomarker for lipid peroxidation of cells (Medina et al., 2017). Compared with the control, while FCNs reduce the MDA levels in water hyacinth leaves, N-FCNs increase MDA contents (Fig. 3i). It indicates that FCNs can protect cells from the oxidative damage, but N-FCNs aggravate the oxidative stress caused by HMs. Antioxidant enzymes, such as POD, SOD and CAT, can protect cells from adverse effects of the reactive oxygen species (ROS) (Hu et al., 2018). Antioxidant enzymes activity, meanwhile, are up- or down-regulated to cope with the serious oxidative damage caused by HMs and eliminate the metals toxicity (Saleh et al., 2020; Cao et al., 2017). SOD is a major O2 .- scavenger and its enzymatic action results in H2O2 and O2 formation, and then the produced H2O2 is scavenged by CAT and POD (Mostafa, 2009). In terms of SOD levels in water hyacinth leaves, irrespective of FCNs or N-FCNs, Pb significantly increases SOD levels at a concentration of 1.0 mg L−1 (Fig. 3k). Cd2+ at 0.1 mg L−1 has no remarkable influence on SOD level. With addition of FCNs or N-FCNs, both of them significantly decrease the SOD activity under the HMs stress, indicating that the amendments reduce the oxidative damage caused by HMs. These phenomena were also found in wheat treated by both carbon dots and Cd2+ (Lian et al., 2020). Compared with Pb1 or CT groups, Cd at 0.01 mg L−1 increases POD content in leaves (Fig. 3j). After treating by FCNs or N-FCNs, the POD levels are further improved under Pb2+ (1.0 mg L−1) stress or in the normal condition. However, N-FCNs decrease the enzyme activity under Cd2+ (0.1 mg L−1) stress when FCNs have no effects on the POD activity. Whether it is Pb2+, Cd2+, FCNs or N-FCNs, all of them can significantly increase the CAT activity in water hyacinth (Fig. 3l). The activity of CAT is maximumly promoted by FCNs and N-FCNs under Pb2+ or Cd2+ stress, which is just the opposite of the POD response. The decreased SOD activity demonstrates that the O2 .– level and the produced H2O2 level in leaves are lower. The improvement of the CAT activity promotes the elimination of H2O2, thus enhances the oxidation resistance of plant cells. As a result, the altered enzymatic levels can protect the membrane stability and scavenge the oxidative damage (O2 .– and H2O2 production) induced by HMs, indicating the tolerance of plants against HMs stress (Saleh et al., 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of FCNs/N-FCNs on nutrient uptake in water hyacinth
ce:section.ce:para: Besides HMs, CNMs can also affect the nutrients uptake by the plant in water (Hu et al., 2018). Cd2+ and Pb2+ are absorbed across the plasmalemma of root cells by channels of essential elements, such as depolarization-activated calcium channels for Pb2+, and divalent transition metal transporters for Cd2+ (Kushwaha et al., 2018). Cu, Zn, Fe and Mn levels in water hyacinth root were investigated to investigate the nutrient uptake under FCNs or N-FCNs exposure. From the results shown in Fig. 4 and Table S7, the four nutrients levels are different as the results of Pb, Cd stress or FCNs, N-FCNs additive. Under Pb (1.0 mg L−1) stress, Zn and Cu levels in root significantly raise with FCNs and N-FCNs. In contrast, Fe level decreases and Mn level has no significant change. However, only Zn level in root is promoted by FCNs and N-FCNs when water hyacinth is exposed to Cd2+ (0.1 mg L−1). All of the other three nutrients’ (Cu, Fe and Mn) levels are decreased by FCNs and N-FCNs, probably due to Cd2+ occupying the divalent transition metal transporters. It is worth noticing that Fe content in the root of each group is extremely high and even exceeds the normal level (Wu et al., 2011), which may induce secondary toxicity to the plant growth. However, the presence of FCNs and N-FCNs appears to neutralize the Fe level under HMs stress, thus alleviates the synergy toxic of over increased Cd and Fe. Based on the results, FCNs and N-FCNs can “smartly” regulate the microelement uptake by the root to promote the plant growth or mitigate the stress.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of FCNs/N-FCNs on the gene expression
ce:section.ce:para: Higher plants can perceive the environmental signals and then transferred to the relevant target cells during growth. The induced intracellular signal transduction can regulate the expression of genes or change the activity of the enzyme (Scheres and Van der Putten, 2017). Toxic HM ions directly or indirectly influence DNA synthesis and expression. This is mainly because HMs ions can interact with DNA through hydrogen bonding of the metal-coordinating water molecules resulting in the disruption of the base pair hydrogen bonding and cause changes in the structure of the DNA double helix. Besides, ROS generated by metal catalyzed reactions can occur serious oxidative damage to genes structure and function (Theophanides and Anastassopoulou, 2017). However, FCNs-based nanomaterials can immobilize HMs ions, stabilize the enzyme structure and scavenge ROS (Chen et al., 2020c), thus alleviate the negative effects of HMs on DNA structure and expression. FCNs-based materials show significantly enhancement on Pb/Cd tolerance and accumulation in A. thaliana, which are reflected in Fig. S11 and our previous reports (Chen et al., 2020b). The effects of FCNs and N-FCNs on the expression of the A. thaliana PLANT DEFENSIN 2 gene AtPDF2.5, which triggers the Cd2+ detoxification, accumulation and transport, are highlighted in Fig. S12. The figures show that although AtPDF2.5 can be overexpressed by FCNs, it is significantly down-regulated by N-FCNs. From the results, FCNs can enhance the plant tolerance and bio-adsorption to Cd2+ partly by up-regulation of AtPDF2.5. In addition, the down-expressed AtPDF2.5 by N-FCNs partly contributes to the lower transport factor of HMs in N-FCNs treatments. As different species of plants have various genetic responses to different HMs, more studies on the effect of FCNs and N-FCNs on related genes are expected.

Based on above results, the FCNs-based nanomaterials promote the bioaccumulation effect of plant may due to the following reasons: Firstly, FCNs-based nanomaterials can transport ions into plant due to their adsorption and transfer function. Secondly, the materials can induce vigorous roots and promote the root absorptive capacity. Thirdly, FCNs materials can promote transpiration of leaves to accelerate the ions transport with water. Fourthly, genes related to heavy metal adsorption and transport were regulated by the FCN-based materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of FCNs, N-FCNs and conventional CNMs on water hyacinth responding to combined pollution with Pb2+ and Cd2+
ce:section.ce:para: Combined pollution caused by multiple HMs is one of the important environmental pollution forms. Compared with the single metal, multiple HMs behave interactively and show combined ecological effects, making the environmental remediation challengeable (Egodawatta et al., 2020). For example, some studies showed that contaminants below their ‘no observed effect’ concentrations might cause toxicity when present in mixtures (Egodawatta et al., 2018, 2020). In this part, the influence of FCNs and N-FCNs on the laws of HMs migration and accumulation in water hyacinth are presented in Fig. 5 .

By comparing the HMs contents in water hyacinth under combined stress to those under single Pb2+ or Cd2+ stress without nanodots, Cd contents in vivo and Pb contents in root are reduced (Fig. 5a), while Pb contents in shoot are increased by 2.1 times. In the Pb–Cd combined polluted water, although the Pb and Cd removal efficiencies are decreased by 22.7% and 44.4% (Fig. 5dande), the TFs of both Pb and Cd are dramatically improved to 6.1 and 2.7 times (Fig. S13), respectively, comparing to the single Pb2+ or Cd2+ pollution. These results confirm that Pb2+ and Cd2+ have antagonistic effects on bioaccumulation of water hyacinth to the single metal, especially a competitive advantage of Pb2+ over Cd2+ in their sorption by the water hyacinth roots.

The additive of FCNs and N-FCNs dramatically improve the Cd contents in the shoot by 64.9% and 21.0%, as well as in the root by 77.5% and 25.1%, respectively. Although N-FCNs improve Pb contents in the root and shoot to 3.0 and 1.2 times, FCNs increase the shoot Pb content by 83.2% and decrease the root Pb content by 24.2%. Consequently, while BF of Cd reduces under the influence of FCNs or N-FCNs, that of Pb is increased by FCNs and reduced by N-FCNs. The results suggest that FCNs and N-FCNs preferentially absorb and transfer Pb ions rather than Cd ions in water hyacinth. Because of the higher HMs contents in the plant, faster plant growth and roots development (Fig. 5d, S9), water hyacinth treated by FCNs and N-FCNs has the higher removal efficiency to Pb (1.39 and 1.28 times, respectively) and Cd (1.41 and 1.19 times, respectively) than the control (Fig. 5c). However, chlorophyll content in leaves is not improved by FCNs or N-FCNs, probably because of the synergy toxic effects of Pb–Cd on chloroplast. Based on the above results, FCNs and N-FCNs can cooperate with water hyacinth to raise the removal efficiency of HMs in water, while enhance the plant tolerance under Pb–Cd combined stress. This provide a new idea for the remediation of Pb–Cd combined polluted water areas.

The performance of FCNs and N-FCNs were compared with CNTs and GO in affecting hyperaccumulation of water hyacinth under Pb–Cd stress (Fig. S14). Although the Pb and Cd removal efficiency of all the CNMs can reach 98%, the HMs contents in water hyacinth of each group are of significant difference. When FCNs remove HMs from water through the synergistic effect of materials adsorption and enhanced bioremediation (Fig. S14c, d), CNTs mainly depends on the adsorption of materials. The significantly increased HMs contents in plant treated by GO were mainly due to the lower biomass caused by plant growth retardation. Despite the high adsorption capacity, CNTs or GO chelated with HMs may form precipitate and stay in natural water. Then additional measures are needed to remove the chelates mixing in the sediment to avoid secondary contamination. However, the FCNs (N-FCNs)-HMs chelates can be adsorbed by roots because of their smaller sizes and good dispersion (Chen et al., 2020b). Therefore, FCNs has better prospects for environmental application than other conventional CNMs, considering the efficiency, convenience and plant friendly under HMs stress.

In conclusion, we demonstrate that both FCNs and N-FCNs can act as exogenous signals to affect the bioaccumulation and growth of water hyacinth either directly or indirectly via regulating physiological activities or altering the pollutants behavior. FCNs-based materials show the high adsorption capacity to HMs, due to their nanoscale and high content of electronegative functional groups on the surface and at the edge of the carbon skeleton. FCNs and N-FCNs adsorb Pb2+ and Cd2+ mainly through ion exchange, complexation and electrostatic attraction. Comparing with FCNs, the carboxyl and amino groups further increase the bonding sites and improve the adsorption capacity of N-FCNs to Pb2+ and Cd2+. Based on their excellent adsorption capacity, FCNs-based materials can not only act as transportations to deliver more HMs ions into the plant, and thus promote the HMs bioaccumulation and transfer in the plant, but also immobilize HMs ions to moderate their biotoxicity or inhibit their transfer. Consequently, FCNs and N-FCNs facilitate the removal efficiency of HMs by water hyacinth in a short contact time, however, amplified HMs toxicity to plant over a longer period of time. FCNs-based materials can promote plant growth and development under Pb or (and) Cd stress in many cases, predominantly ascribed to the “smart” uptake and delivery of HMs and micronutrient, improved chlorophyll level, activated antioxidant defense system and regulation of related gene expression. However, further studies are still needed at genetic and molecular level for the better understanding of increase in the tolerance mechanisms of FCNs-based materials against Pb and Cd toxicity in plants. This study suggests that FCNs and N-FCNs could cooperate with water hyacinth to raise the bioremediation efficiency to HMs in water, as well as enhance the plant tolerance under the Pb–Cd combined stress, which paving the way for applying FCNs-based nanomaterials and water hyacinth as the synergistic remediation strategy for HMs pollutants in water environment.
